items.title: Media reports: Hamas recalls 7,000 fighters to reassert control over parts of Gaza | DW News
items.author: DW News
items.content:
<article>Huge crowds have gathered in Tel Avivâ€™s Hostage Square, a focal point of protest and remembrance since the October 7 attacks.
 
Families of those taken captive by Hamas have rallied there throughout the war. As a ceasefire with Hamas holds for a second day, US envoy Steve Witkoff addressed the crowd to cheers. Protesters hope it will be their last gathering, with Israelâ€™s military saying the remaining hostages could be released by Monday's noon deadline. Of the 48 still held in Gaza, only about 20 are thought to be alive.  

Meanwhile in Gaza, hundreds of thousands of Palestinians are returning to what's left of their homes as Hamas' ceasefire with Israel holds. Aid deliveries to the Strip are expected to ramp up beginning Sunday. 

00:00 Gazan's return to the rubble of their homes
03:28 Mirette F. Mabrouk, Middle East Institute
07:36 Ahmed Bayram, Norwegian Refugee Council

For more news go to: http://www.dw.com/en/

items.title: A Gaza womanâ€™s journey back home | DW News
items.author: DW News
items.content:
<article>This displaced Palestinian woman is on the road back to Gaza City. With 90% of residential buildings across the strip destroyed or damaged by the war according to UN estimates, she knows she may be returning to a house in ruins, but the longing to go home is stronger than the fear of what's awaiting her.

items.title: Gaza ceasefire deal: What to expect next
items.author: DW News
items.content:
<article>The clock is ticking on the deadline for Hamas to release the remaining hostages in Gaza - both alive and dead. The militants have until noon on Monday to free them. With a ceasefire now in place, and the partial withdrawal of Israeli troops, hundreds of thousands of Palestinians have been moving back to the northern Gaza Strip in the hope that the peace will last. Aid deliveries to Gaza are expected to be scaled up beginning Sunday.

00:00 Monday deadline for release of Israeli hostages from Gaza
03:38 DW's Tania KrĂ¤mer reports from Jerusalem
08:47 DW speaks with Yossi Mekelberg, associate fellow at Chatham House and a Middle East analyst
14:31 DW speaks with Tess Ingram, UNICEF Communications Manager at UNICEF and is in Al Mawasi in the Gaza Strip

items.title: Chaos in France as Macron reappoint Lecornu days after he quit | DW News
items.author: DW News
items.content:
<article>French President Emmanuel Macron has reappointed Sebastien Lecornu as prime minister - just days after he quit over political infighting. Lecornu must form a government and present a new budget to a hung parliament by Monday. Opposition lawmakers have criticized his reappointment, with parties calling for snap elections and Macron's resignation. Divisions over reducing France's soaring debt have toppled Lecornu's two predecessors.

00:00 Macron reappoints Lecornu as PM in France
00:31 DW speaks with journalist Anne-Elisabeth Moutet in Paris

For more news go to: http://www.dw.com/en/

items.title: Will 92-year old Paul Biya extend his grip on power in Cameroon? | DW News
items.author: DW News
items.content:
<article>All eyes are on Cameroon, as millions of voters head to the polls on October 12 to decide who will lead the country. Will 92-year-old President Paul Biya, 
the world's oldest non-royal head of state, extend his four-decade grip on power? Biya has ruled since 1982, and faces a almost a dozen challengers. But critics wonder if this will be a real test or just a formality.

For more news go to: http://www.dw.com/en/

items.author: DW News
items.content:
<article>More than 200 Vietnamese trainees at a vocational training college in Berlin have dropped out and disappeared. Campaigners fear they've become victims of modern slavery, and say a 2024 deal between Germany and Vietnam to bring skilled workers here has failed them.

items.title: Authorities are trying to stop Boluarte from leaving Peru | DW News
items.author: DW News
items.content:
<article>Public discontent over corruption and a surge in violent crime have plunged Peru into a political crisis. 
In an overnight congressional impeachment session, lawmakers removed the unpopular President Dina Boluarte from office. Peru's new leader is Jose Jeri, a 38-year-old lawyer with little political experience. Jeri has now been sworn in and has vowed to tackle crime, seek national reconciliation, and ensure that fair elections are held next April. 

Chapters:
0:00 Crime wave plunges Peru into political crisis
0:39 JosĂ© JerĂ­, Peruvian President
1:06 Martin Riepl, Journalist
